failure_surface: Test case evaluation returned 0, indicating the output workbook did not contain expected data in the target range Sheet1!H1:I370 despite code execution completing without errors.
root_cause: The agent's code had a variable scoping bug (using undefined 'i' instead of 'j' in column assignment) combined with premature loop termination ('break' after first match found), resulting in incomplete data population. The agent also assumed column headers existed without verifying their presence or discovering actual column indices dynamically.
minimal_fix_hypothesis: Remove premature break statements when multiple matches may exist per source item; verify target worksheet headers exist before referencing them by name; fix variable scope issues in nested loops by using consistent loop variables.
evidence: Code used 'break' statement after finding first match, preventing processing of additional matching rows if multiple race numbers exist per meet
Variable 'i' referenced in ws1.cell(row=target_row, column=i+1) was defined in outer enumerate context but used inside inner loop where 'j' was the active counter
Agent directly accessed h1.index('Meet') and h2.index('Track') without checking if those header strings exist in the actual spreadsheet
Code executed without raising exceptions (returned True) but evaluation showed complete test failure, indicating silent logic errors rather than crashes

memory_items.title: Avoid Premature Loop Termination
memory_items.lesson: Do not use 'break' in nested loops when you need to process all matching items, especially when the target range requires multiple rows to be filled.
memory_items.generalization: In spreadsheet manipulation tasks where one source record maps to multiple target cells, continue processing all relevant source rows rather than stopping after the first match.

memory_items.title: Verify Header Existence Before Accessing
memory_items.lesson: Always confirm required column headers exist in the worksheet before calling .index() or similar methods, otherwise IndexError will occur silently or headers may be misplaced.
memory_items.generalization: Before referencing column positions by header name, validate that the header string exists in the first row and handle missing headers gracefully with fallback logic.

memory_items.title: Debug Variable Scope in Nested Loops
memory_items.lesson: When writing nested loops for spreadsheet operations, ensure loop variables are used consistently within their proper scope to avoid referencing undefined or stale variables.
memory_items.generalization: In multi-level iterations common in sheet-to-sheet data mapping, trace variable usage to confirm each reference resolves to the intended index value from the correct loop level.

edits.content:
## Error Patterns to Avoid

### Premature Loop Termination
- Do not use `break` statements in nested iteration when the target requires multiple data points from source matches.
- When mapping one-source-to-many-target scenarios, process all matching rows before moving to the next source item.

### Header Validation Before Reference
- Always validate that required column headers exist in the worksheet before calling `.index()` or accessing by name.
- Handle missing headers with graceful fallbacks rather than assuming headers are present.

### Variable Scope Consistency
- Track loop variable usage carefully across nested iterations to avoid referencing variables from wrong scope levels.
- Use descriptive variable names that indicate their iteration depth (e.g., `row_target`, `row_source`).

### Silent Failure Detection
- Execution completion (return value True) does not guarantee correct data population.
- Add explicit validation steps to verify the target range contains expected data after modification.
